title: Tonsillitis & Pharyngitis
oneLiner: Acute infection of pharynx/tonsils; most viral, but Group A Streptococcus (GAS) needs treatment to prevent suppurative & non-suppurative complications.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction (peritonsillar abscess / epiglottitis / retropharyngeal abscess):** The single finding – **trismus** (inability to open mouth fully) + **muffled “hot potato” voice** indicates abscess; **stridor**, drooling, tripod positioning suggests epiglottitis. Requires immediate ENT anaesthetic review.
- **Lemierre’s syndrome:** The single finding – **severe unilateral neck pain/swelling** + **persistent high fever** despite antibiotics, often with rigors and respiratory distress. Look for tender, indurated neck vein (jugular thrombophlebitis). Blood cultures may be positive for *Fusobacterium necrophorum*.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** sore throat (often severe), odynophagia (pain on swallowing), dysphagia, pyrexia, malaise.
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Pharyngeal erythema, tonsillar hypertrophy, exudate (white/pustular patches).
    - Cervical lymphadenopathy (tender, ± anterior or posterior).
    - Palatal petechiae (more common in EBV – infectious mononucleosis).
    - Trismus, muffled voice, uvular deviation → suspect peritonsillar abscess.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → Least likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
    - **Viral pharyngitis (>80%):** Cough, rhinorrhoea, hoarseness, conjunctivitis – not typical for GAS.
    - **Group A Streptococcal tonsillopharyngitis:** Sudden onset, high fever, tender anterior cervical nodes, exudate; Centor score ≥3.
    - **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV):** Posterior cervical nodes (especially bilateral), splenomegaly, palatal petechiae, fatigue >1 week, ± rash with amoxicillin.
    - **Suppurative complications:** Peritonsillar abscess (trismus, muffled voice, uvula deviated away), retropharyngeal abscess (neck hyperextension, dysphagia, drooling).
    - **Rare but don’t miss:** Diphtheria (grey pseudomembrane, bull neck, low fever – very rare in UK/IE), HIV seroconversion (mucocutaneous ulcers, lymphadenopathy, rash).
- **Splitter:** Centor score ≥3 (T >38°C, lymphadenopathy, no cough, exudate) strongly favours bacterial GAS; no temperature spikes, cough present, rhinorrhoea → viral.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Centor scoring (0–4) or FeverPAIN scoring (0–5) – use to guide antibiotic decision. Consider rapid antigen detection test if available (quick + high specificity).
- **Bloods:** CRP (may be elevated in bacterial, but also in EBV), FBC (lymphocytosis in EBV), monospot/EBV serology if suspected infectious mononucleosis (avoid amoxicillin if positive).
- **Imaging:** Not routinely needed. If abscess suspected (trismus, unilateral swelling) → emergency **CT neck with IV contrast** (or ultrasound if limited) to define collection for drainage.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
    - **Centor criteria** (modified for UK use): fever >38°C, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudate, absence of cough. Score ≥3 → higher probability of GAS.
    - **FeverPAIN criteria:** Fever >38°C, purulence, attend within 3 days of symptoms, inflamed tonsils, no cough or coryza. Score ≥4 suggests treat.
- **Never-miss test:** **Throat swab for microbiology** (culture) if Centor ≥3 or high-risk groups (history of rheumatic fever, valvular heart disease, recurrent severe episodes). Also **monospot** if EBV suspected (especially if posterior cervical nodes + fatigue) – omission leads to misprescribing amoxicillin and causing rash.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Analgesia: Paracetamol 1g PO/IV qds + ibuprofen 400mg PO tds (or alternative NSAID) – both for sore throat.
    - Consider throat swab & start antibiotics if Centor ≥3 (or FeverPAIN ≥4) – but can also use next-day rash decision if low risk.
    - If stridor/tripoding/drooling: call ENT + anaesthetics immediately, keep NBM, give IV access, high-flow oxygen, consider IV dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg (max 10mg) for airway oedema.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - IV fluids if unable to swallow (e.g. abscess, severe odynophagia) – maintain hydration.
    - IV antibiotics if oral not tolerated or severe: Benzylpenicillin 1.2g QDS IV (or clindamycin 600mg QDS IV if penicillin allergy).
    - If abscess confirmed: arrange drainage (needle aspiration or incision & drainage) under ENT.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Complete 10-day course of oral antibiotics for GAS (or 5-day if using amoxicillin? Check local guidelines – NICE recommends 10/5 days for penicillin/amoxicillin respectively).
    - For recurrent tonsillitis (≥7 episodes in 1 year, or ≥5 in 2 years): discuss tonsillectomy (SIGN guideline).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1–2L/day (or Hartmann’s) if NBM or poor oral intake.
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Consider if prolonged immobility or dehydration.
    - **Diet:** NBM only if suspicion of abscess or airway compromise; otherwise soft/liquid diet.
    - **Observations:** NEWS2: minimum 4-hourly; if sepsis/severe features, consider continuous or 1-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line for GAS (confirmed or high suspicion):**
    - Phenoxymethylpenicillin (Penicillin V) 500mg PO QDS for 10 days (children: 12.5mg/kg QDS).
    - Alternative: Amoxicillin 500mg PO TDS for 10 days (adults) – but **avoid if infectious mononucleosis suspected** (causes morbilliform rash).
- **Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic):**
    - Clarithromycin 500mg PO BD for 5 days (or azithromycin 500mg OD for 3 days).
    - Or clindamycin 600mg PO QDS for 5 days.
- **IV (severe disease):**
    - Benzylpenicillin 1.2g QDS IV (or 2.4g QDS if sepsis) until oral tolerated.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Penicillins: history of immediate hypersensitivity.
    - Clarithromycin: increases effect of statins, warfarin (need monitoring), risk of QT prolongation.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Penicillins: minimal adjustment except high doses in severe renal failure.
    - Clarithromycin: reduce dose in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min: give 250mg BD).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving amoxicillin when EBV infection is present** – causes a classic non-allergic rash that may be mistaken for drug allergy, leading to label and wrong antibiotic choices. **Always check monospot before starting amoxicillin in adolescents/young adults with posterior cervical nodes and fatigue.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - **Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy):** Trismus, muffled voice, uvula deviation → needs drainage + IV antibiotics.
    - **Retropharyngeal abscess:** Neck hyperextension, drooling, stridor → airway emergency.
    - **Lemierre’s syndrome:** Septic thrombophlebitis of internal jugular vein ± metastatic abscess (lungs, joints) – high mortality without antibiotics.
    - **Acute rheumatic fever** (rare in UK/IE but still occurs; preventable with treatment of GAS).
- **Chronic:**
    - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (1–3 weeks after infection).
    - Recurrent tonsillitis ± obstructive sleep apnoea (if tonsillar hypertrophy persists).
sections.Prognosis:
- Viral pharyngitis: self-limiting within 7–10 days.
- GAS tonsillopharyngitis: if treated, symptoms improve in 24–48h; untreated suppurative complications occur in ~1% (abscess, Lemierre’s). Non-suppurative (rheumatic fever) can occur 2–3 weeks later – incidence ~1–3 per 100,000 in UK.
- Mortality: extremely low in immunocompetent patients, but high if Lemierre’s syndrome missed (up to 10% despite treatment).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Use Centor score** in all adults to decide antibiotic treatment – reduce unnecessary prescribing.
- **Always examine for trismus and muffled voice** before prescribing steroids; if present, suspect abscess and call ENT.
- **Check monospot** in young adults with bilateral posterior cervical nodes, fatigue, or palatal petechiae before starting amoxicillin – it changes management.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT prescribe amoxicillin** without ruling out EBV in younger patients – the resulting rash can be mistaken for drug allergy and lead to lifelong unnecessary penicillin avoidance.
- **Do NOT ignore trismus** – this is a red flag for abscess; calling ENT late increases risk of airway compromise.
- **Do NOT routinely give antibiotics** for all sore throats – Centor ≤2, no high-risk features: use analgesia alone and safety net.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - Difficulty breathing or drooling (airway compromise).
    - Unable to swallow even saliva, or severe pain despite analgesia.
    - Worsening fever or high fever (>38.5°C) after 48h of antibiotics.
    - New unilateral neck swelling, trismus, or muffled voice.
- **Follow-up:**
    - If antibiotics prescribed: complete course; no routine follow-up unless symptoms persist >5 days.
    - If no improvement after 4 days of antibiotics → return for outpatient ENT review ± throat swab (culture for GAS resistance or alternative cause).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Medical registrar / ENT reg) immediately if:**
    - Stridor, drooling, or tripod positioning – suspect epiglottitis or retropharyngeal abscess.
    - Trismus + muffled voice – needs ENT assessment for abscess drainage.
    - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L – start sepsis 6 and escalate.
    - GCS <15 or altered consciousness (rare, but consider sepsis).
- **ICU / Crash team:**
    - Any airway compromise (complete obstruction, impending arrest).

rawMarkdown:
## Tonsillitis & Pharyngitis
**One-liner:** Acute infection of pharynx/tonsils; most viral, but Group A Streptococcus (GAS) needs treatment to prevent suppurative & non-suppurative complications.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction (peritonsillar abscess / epiglottitis / retropharyngeal abscess):** The single finding – **trismus** (inability to open mouth fully) + **muffled “hot potato” voice** indicates abscess; **stridor**, drooling, tripod positioning suggests epiglottitis. Requires immediate ENT anaesthetic review.
- **Lemierre’s syndrome:** The single finding – **severe unilateral neck pain/swelling** + **persistent high fever** despite antibiotics, often with rigors and respiratory distress. Look for tender, indurated neck vein (jugular thrombophlebitis). Blood cultures may be positive for *Fusobacterium necrophorum*.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** sore throat (often severe), odynophagia (pain on swallowing), dysphagia, pyrexia, malaise.
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Pharyngeal erythema, tonsillar hypertrophy, exudate (white/pustular patches).
    - Cervical lymphadenopathy (tender, ± anterior or posterior).
    - Palatal petechiae (more common in EBV – infectious mononucleosis).
    - Trismus, muffled voice, uvular deviation → suspect peritonsillar abscess.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → Least likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
    - **Viral pharyngitis (>80%):** Cough, rhinorrhoea, hoarseness, conjunctivitis – not typical for GAS.
    - **Group A Streptococcal tonsillopharyngitis:** Sudden onset, high fever, tender anterior cervical nodes, exudate; Centor score ≥3.
    - **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV):** Posterior cervical nodes (especially bilateral), splenomegaly, palatal petechiae, fatigue >1 week, ± rash with amoxicillin.
    - **Suppurative complications:** Peritonsillar abscess (trismus, muffled voice, uvula deviated away), retropharyngeal abscess (neck hyperextension, dysphagia, drooling).
    - **Rare but don’t miss:** Diphtheria (grey pseudomembrane, bull neck, low fever – very rare in UK/IE), HIV seroconversion (mucocutaneous ulcers, lymphadenopathy, rash).
- **Splitter:** Centor score ≥3 (T >38°C, lymphadenopathy, no cough, exudate) strongly favours bacterial GAS; no temperature spikes, cough present, rhinorrhoea → viral.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Centor scoring (0–4) or FeverPAIN scoring (0–5) – use to guide antibiotic decision. Consider rapid antigen detection test if available (quick + high specificity).
- **Bloods:** CRP (may be elevated in bacterial, but also in EBV), FBC (lymphocytosis in EBV), monospot/EBV serology if suspected infectious mononucleosis (avoid amoxicillin if positive).
- **Imaging:** Not routinely needed. If abscess suspected (trismus, unilateral swelling) → emergency **CT neck with IV contrast** (or ultrasound if limited) to define collection for drainage.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
    - **Centor criteria** (modified for UK use): fever >38°C, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudate, absence of cough. Score ≥3 → higher probability of GAS.
    - **FeverPAIN criteria:** Fever >38°C, purulence, attend within 3 days of symptoms, inflamed tonsils, no cough or coryza. Score ≥4 suggests treat.
- **Never-miss test:** **Throat swab for microbiology** (culture) if Centor ≥3 or high-risk groups (history of rheumatic fever, valvular heart disease, recurrent severe episodes). Also **monospot** if EBV suspected (especially if posterior cervical nodes + fatigue) – omission leads to misprescribing amoxicillin and causing rash.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Analgesia: Paracetamol 1g PO/IV qds + ibuprofen 400mg PO tds (or alternative NSAID) – both for sore throat.
    - Consider throat swab & start antibiotics if Centor ≥3 (or FeverPAIN ≥4) – but can also use next-day rash decision if low risk.
    - If stridor/tripoding/drooling: call ENT + anaesthetics immediately, keep NBM, give IV access, high-flow oxygen, consider IV dexamethasone 0.15mg/kg (max 10mg) for airway oedema.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - IV fluids if unable to swallow (e.g. abscess, severe odynophagia) – maintain hydration.
    - IV antibiotics if oral not tolerated or severe: Benzylpenicillin 1.2g QDS IV (or clindamycin 600mg QDS IV if penicillin allergy).
    - If abscess confirmed: arrange drainage (needle aspiration or incision & drainage) under ENT.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Complete 10-day course of oral antibiotics for GAS (or 5-day if using amoxicillin? Check local guidelines – NICE recommends 10/5 days for penicillin/amoxicillin respectively).
    - For recurrent tonsillitis (≥7 episodes in 1 year, or ≥5 in 2 years): discuss tonsillectomy (SIGN guideline).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1–2L/day (or Hartmann’s) if NBM or poor oral intake.
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Consider if prolonged immobility or dehydration.
    - **Diet:** NBM only if suspicion of abscess or airway compromise; otherwise soft/liquid diet.
    - **Observations:** NEWS2: minimum 4-hourly; if sepsis/severe features, consider continuous or 1-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line for GAS (confirmed or high suspicion):**
    - Phenoxymethylpenicillin (Penicillin V) 500mg PO QDS for 10 days (children: 12.5mg/kg QDS).
    - Alternative: Amoxicillin 500mg PO TDS for 10 days (adults) – but **avoid if infectious mononucleosis suspected** (causes morbilliform rash).
- **Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic):**
    - Clarithromycin 500mg PO BD for 5 days (or azithromycin 500mg OD for 3 days).
    - Or clindamycin 600mg PO QDS for 5 days.
- **IV (severe disease):**
    - Benzylpenicillin 1.2g QDS IV (or 2.4g QDS if sepsis) until oral tolerated.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Penicillins: history of immediate hypersensitivity.
    - Clarithromycin: increases effect of statins, warfarin (need monitoring), risk of QT prolongation.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Penicillins: minimal adjustment except high doses in severe renal failure.
    - Clarithromycin: reduce dose in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min: give 250mg BD).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving amoxicillin when EBV infection is present** – causes a classic non-allergic rash that may be mistaken for drug allergy, leading to label and wrong antibiotic choices. **Always check monospot before starting amoxicillin in adolescents/young adults with posterior cervical nodes and fatigue.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - **Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy):** Trismus, muffled voice, uvula deviation → needs drainage + IV antibiotics.
    - **Retropharyngeal abscess:** Neck hyperextension, drooling, stridor → airway emergency.
    - **Lemierre’s syndrome:** Septic thrombophlebitis of internal jugular vein ± metastatic abscess (lungs, joints) – high mortality without antibiotics.
    - **Acute rheumatic fever** (rare in UK/IE but still occurs; preventable with treatment of GAS).
- **Chronic:**
    - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (1–3 weeks after infection).
    - Recurrent tonsillitis ± obstructive sleep apnoea (if tonsillar hypertrophy persists).

### Prognosis
- Viral pharyngitis: self-limiting within 7–10 days.
- GAS tonsillopharyngitis: if treated, symptoms improve in 24–48h; untreated suppurative complications occur in ~1% (abscess, Lemierre’s). Non-suppurative (rheumatic fever) can occur 2–3 weeks later – incidence ~1–3 per 100,000 in UK.
- Mortality: extremely low in immunocompetent patients, but high if Lemierre’s syndrome missed (up to 10% despite treatment).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Use Centor score** in all adults to decide antibiotic treatment – reduce unnecessary prescribing.
- **Always examine for trismus and muffled voice** before prescribing steroids; if present, suspect abscess and call ENT.
- **Check monospot** in young adults with bilateral posterior cervical nodes, fatigue, or palatal petechiae before starting amoxicillin – it changes management.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT prescribe amoxicillin** without ruling out EBV in younger patients – the resulting rash can be mistaken for drug allergy and lead to lifelong unnecessary penicillin avoidance.
- **Do NOT ignore trismus** – this is a red flag for abscess; calling ENT late increases risk of airway compromise.
- **Do NOT routinely give antibiotics** for all sore throats – Centor ≤2, no high-risk features: use analgesia alone and safety net.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - Difficulty breathing or drooling (airway compromise).
    - Unable to swallow even saliva, or severe pain despite analgesia.
    - Worsening fever or high fever (>38.5°C) after 48h of antibiotics.
    - New unilateral neck swelling, trismus, or muffled voice.
- **Follow-up:**
    - If antibiotics prescribed: complete course; no routine follow-up unless symptoms persist >5 days.
    - If no improvement after 4 days of antibiotics → return for outpatient ENT review ± throat swab (culture for GAS resistance or alternative cause).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Medical registrar / ENT reg) immediately if:**
    - Stridor, drooling, or tripod positioning – suspect epiglottitis or retropharyngeal abscess.
    - Trismus + muffled voice – needs ENT assessment for abscess drainage.
    - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L – start sepsis 6 and escalate.
    - GCS <15 or altered consciousness (rare, but consider sepsis).
- **ICU / Crash team:**
    - Any airway compromise (complete obstruction, impending arrest).
